请将“The act of money-making and the act of governing represent a division of labour in society. Unless the state is given some autonomy, it cannot work for the common affairs of the money-making class and for hegemonic fractions of this class. Nor can it perform functions in support of the general affairs of society which are important whether or not a society is capitalist (e.g. dealing with a pandemic). Indeed, regardless of the class character of state actors, ‘the role of the state in administering or overseeing a large-scale national community ensures that certain of its activities (e.g. upholding certain kinds of criminal law or traffic regulations such as the Highway Code) must be “classless” in the sense that they are indispensable to the running of any industrialised urban society’ (Creaven, 2000: 268). The fact that a part of society — the capitalist state — needs to look after the common affairs of the society as a whole (these are not necessarily the same as the common affairs of the capitalist class) contributes to, and requires, a degree of autonomy of the state. This autonomy is then used by the ruling class and is given a class colour: when the state does operate on behalf of an entire capitalist society, this functioning is mediated by capitalist class interests. This is seen, for example, in the relation between the state’s attempt to keep society ‘crime free’ and the prison-industrial complex, or between the state’s attempt to defend a country against foreign enemies and the military-industrial complex, or indeed between the state’s attempt to fight a pandemic and the healthcare-industrial complex that churns billionaires when millions die. The so-called class-neutral functions of the capitalist state end up having a class character, because the state is inherently the capitalist state.
Generally speaking, given the conflicts of interests among capitalists, self-interested capitalists as capitalists might not be great administrators to look after the common affairs of the capitalist class as a whole. There are all kinds of conflicts within the capitalist class within a country and globally. Within a country, there are different competing ‘regional group interests, different industrial interests, antagonisms within an industry, rivalry between producers of consumers’ and producers’ goods, light and heavy industry, and so on, aside from religious, political, and other ideological differences’ (Draper, 1977: 323). This fact ‘makes it more difficult for any individual capitalist to be trusted as executor for the class as a whole’ (ibid.). For example, during times of social upheavals from below, ‘concessions may have to be made to the class enemy below, the working classes’ (ibid.). But if concessions have to be made, who will pay for them? At a given point, to the extent that state’s revenue is a given, if more goes to the masses, which section of the dominant class is made to sacrifice and how? It would be difficult for capitalists acting as state actors to decide. Therefore, a ‘built-in characteristic of capitalism . . . minimizes the functioning of capitalists themselves as state administrators or managers’, and there is a need for ‘finding political leaders who can take, and stick to, an overall and farsighted view of the interests and needs of the system as a whole’ (p. 324).
However, one should not have too dim a view of capitalists being able to manage the common affairs of society as well as functions in support of the capitalist class including its hegemonic fractions. One has to just consider how many capitalists increasingly occupy important positions within the state. So the inability of the capitalist class for its self-rule is only relative, just as the autonomy of the state actors qua state actors is relative.9
As indicated already, while the state’s main task is to look after the common economic and political affairs of the capitalists, the very idea of common affairs implies that there are different interests too, and this implies a degree of autonomy on the part of the state. That is, to be able to manage the common affairs, the state has to have a degree of autonomy vis-à-vis short-term interests of capital as a whole and vis-à-vis short-term or long-term interests of specific capitalists (this is acknowledged even by scholars such as Miliband). The fact that there is competition in the economic sphere among capitalists (or indeed between capitalists and landlords as in many LDCs) does require that the state as a structure of relations have a degree of autonomy to be able to resolve conflicts among them.10 It is the autonomy of this structure of relations that is enjoyed by state actors, the bearers of that structure, and their autonomy in turn influences state functions.
A bureaucrat/politician, who is a mere rolling stone, and who lacks his/her own voice, like a president of a chamber of commerce, would not be taken seriously. Even the most obedient servant needs a degree of autonomy which the master is happy to provide, because otherwise the master will be using much time and resource in directing the servant at every step. Indeed, to be able to function in a context where capitalists themselves may not agree on what a good policy might be when, for example, it comes to foreign investment in insurance or industrialization in indigenous areas and so on, a degree of autonomy on the part of the structure of the state and state actors is necessary. In fact, in certain conditions acting as representatives of the private property owners, the bureaucracy can create specific types of private property owners such as the capitalist class.11
It is not just that interests of competing capitalists are different. Capitalists’ interests, at a fundamental level, are incompatible with those of the common people, so there is an objective need to represent what are the interests of capitalists as the interests of the nation as a whole. Here the ideas of influential state actors (officials and politicians) about the national interest and how it might overlap with the interest of the capitalist class are crucial. (In this context, Miliband’s partnership idea is a useful one.)
For the state to operate in the interest of the capitalist class (and of society as a whole), it needs resources (in the form of taxes and the like). An enormous amount of value (social wealth) passes through the state (almost a third of the GDP of countries), giving enormous economic power to the state vis-à-vis any individual capitalist or group of capitalists. This economic power, which is a part of the basis for its autonomy, is enjoyed by the state actors.
While ultimately capitalists’ action is driven by the fact that they have to make an average rate of profit and a significant mass of profit, state actors’ action is driven by the fact that they must ensure reproduction of capitalist relations and help capitalists accumulate value. Yet, within these limits, state actors have their own interests to pursue too. These include careerism, accumulation of wealth in extra-economic ways — through collaboration with capitalists and large-scale landowners, winning elections, or obtaining some form of legitimation from common people in support of their rule.12 Marx (1843) writes, ‘As far as the individual bureaucrat is concerned, the end of the state becomes his private end: a pursuit of higher posts, the building of a career.’ State actors do pursue their own interests because doing so is beneficial to them. They can pursue their interests because the structure of the state has given them some autonomy to. The state bureaucracy might seek to ‘make gains for itself at the expense of others’ (Harman, 1991), that is, capitalists and other groups. Of course, how far they can go in this direction depends on how well capitalists (and others) exercise their agency. In response to state actors actions that might hurt capitalists, capitalists might ‘use their own special position — industrial capital its ability to postpone investment, money capital its ability to move overseas — to fight back’ (Harman, 1991).
In pursuing their own interests, state actors may end up doing things that are not exactly in the capitalists’ short-term interests, and indeed they can pursue their own interests which are relatively independent of capitalists’ inter- ests.13 There can be conflicts, within limits, between autonomously acting state actors and capitalists with their ultimate economic veto power. Certain actors can be a little reformist in relation to the masses and/or may pursue their own interests and may therefore implement policies which may be seen as a drain on the surplus value in the eyes of the capitalists.14 State actors’ perception of what is needed to promote capitalist accumulation may not exactly coincide with the objective interests of the capitalist class and of its individual segments.
State actors, and especially politicians, can be seen as being involved in an M-C-M'-like circuit in politics whereby politicians invest money (M) to bribe poor voters and opposition party representatives and on political propaganda and political violence (e.g. physical coercion against opponents) in order to manufacture a political commodity, called governmental power I which is then used as an instrument to make more money (M') than invested. Similarly, state officials (especially those at the higher echelons) invest money (in the form of bribing politicians and/or other officials) to occupy certain posts that can generate a lot of rent (bribe). So they are also involved in an M-C-M'-like circuit. If capitalists and their political representatives as well as state bureaucrats make more money than invested, where is the money coming from: it ultimately comes from workers/voters not getting the money they need to meet their needs.
There is a limit to the state actors’ self-interested actions; these actions cannot undermine conditions for accumulation too much and too long. These limits are set by capitalism as a structure of relations setting limit on the state as another structure of relations. Accumulation provides employment without which there might be a crisis of legitimation for specific regimes and indeed for the state. Accumulation is a source of taxes and loans for the state itself as long as the state remains bourgeois.15 The consequent pressure on the state as a structure to earn its revenue is manifested as the pressure on the top layer of the bureaucracy: ‘the directing layer in the state bureaucracy is dependent upon successful capitalist exploitation and accumulation’ without which it cannot get the revenues it requires, including ‘for its own privileges and its own functioning’ (Harman, 1991). So state actors are forced to provide conditions that promote capital accumulation within the geographical boundaries of the state. Mandel (1969) says, the fact that capitalist class ‘in large part fills the coffers of the state’ and thus pays ‘for the upkeep of the state’ means that it ‘will demand that the latter place itself completely at their service’ through its laws and institutions.”翻译为中文

赚钱的行为和治理的行为代表了社会分工。除非国家获得一定的自主权，否则它无法为赚钱阶层及其霸权分子的共同事务工作。国家也无法支持对社会普遍重要的共同事务（比如应对大流行）。实际上，不管国家行为者的阶级性质如何，“国家在管理或监督一个大规模国家社区的角色确保了其某些活动（例如，维护某些类型的刑事法律或交通规则，如公路法典）必须是‘无阶级’的，因为这对任何工业化城市社会的运转都是必不可少的”（Creaven, 2000: 268）。一个社会的一部分——资本主义国家——需要照顾整个社会的共同事务（这些事务不一定与资本主义阶级的共同事务相同），这既促成了也需要国家的一定程度的自主权。这种自主性随后被统治阶级利用，并被赋予阶级色彩：当国家代表整个资本主义社会运作时，其功能是通过资本主义阶级利益的调解。例如，在国家试图保持社会“无犯罪”与监狱工业复合体之间的关系，国家试图抵御外敌与军事工业复合体之间的关系，或确实在国家试图对抗大流行与制造亿万富翁的医疗保健工业复合体之间的关系时可以看到这一点，这些是在数以百万计的人死亡时发生的。所谓的资本主义国家的无阶级功能最终具有阶级性质，因为国家本质上是资本主义国家。
一般来说，鉴于资本家之间的利益冲突，以资本家的身份行事的自私的资本家可能不擅长管理资本主义阶级整体的共同事务。在一个国家内部和全球范围内，资本主义阶级内部存在各种冲突。在国家内部，存在不同的竞争“地区集团利益，不同的工业利益，行业内的对立，消费品生产者和生产者之间的竞争，轻工业和重工业等，还有除此之外的宗教、政治和其他意识形态差异”（Draper, 1977: 323）。这一事实“使任何单个资本家被视为整个阶级的执行者变得更加困难”（同上）。例如，在来自下层的社会动荡时期，“可能不得不对下面的阶级敌人，即工人阶级，做出让步”（同上）。但如果必须做出让步，谁来支付费用？在给定的情况下，只要国家的收入是给定的，如果更多的钱流向大众，那么哪一部分占主导地位的阶级被迫牺牲，以及如何牺牲？因此，担任国家行动者的资本家很难决定。因此，资本主义的一个“内在特征……最小化了资本家本身作为国家管理者或经理的运作”，需要“寻找可以从整体和远见的角度考虑系统整体的利益和需求的政治领导人”（第324页）。
然而，不应该过于悲观地看待资本家管理社会共同事务以及支持资本主义阶级及其霸权分子的功能。只需考虑有多少资本家日益在国家内占据重要位置。因此，资本主义阶级自我统治的无能是相对的，正如国家行动者作为国家行动者的自主性也是相对的。正如已经指出的，尽管国家的主要任务是照顾资本家的共同经济和政治事务，但共同事务的概念意味着存在不同的利益，这就暗示了国家在某种程度上的自主性。也就是说，为了能够管理共同事务，国家必须对整个资本的短期利益以及特定资本家的短期或长期利益有一定程度的自主性（甚至像Miliband这样的学者也承认这一点）。资本家之间在经济领域的竞争（或确实资本家与许多最不发达国家的地主之间的竞争）确实要求国家作为一种关系结构具有一定程度的自主性，以能够解决他们之间的冲突。这种关系结构的自主性是由该结构的承载者，即国家行动者享有的，而他们的自主性反过来又影响国家职能。
一个没有自己声音的流浪政治家/官僚，就像一个商会会长，是不会被认真对待的。即使是最服从的仆人也需要一定程度的自主性，而主人乐意提供这种自主性，否则主人将在指导仆人的每一步上花费大量时间和资源。实际上，为了能够在资本家本身可能对什么是好政策（例如，关于保险中的外国投资或在土著地区的工业化等）不一致的情况下发挥作用，国家结构和国家行动者的一定程度的自主性是必要的。实际上，在某些条件下，作为私有财产所有者的代表，官僚机构可以创造特定类型的私有财产所有者，如资本主义阶级。
并不是说竞争资本家的利益不同。从根本上说，资本家的利益与普通人民的利益不兼容，因此客观上需要将资本家的利益作为整个国家的利益来代表。这里，有影响力的国家行动者（官员和政治家）对国家利益以及它可能与资本主义阶级利益的重叠怎样的看法是至关重要的。（在这种情况下，Miliband的伙伴关系想法是一个有用的想法。）
为了让国家能够符合资本主义阶级（和整个社会）的利益，它需要资源（税收等形式）。大量的价值（社会财富）通过国家流动（几乎是国家GDP的三分之一），给予国家相对于任何单个资本家或资本家集团的巨大经济权力。这种经济权力是其自主性基础的一部分，被国家行动者享有。
虽然最终资本家的行动是由他们必须获得平均利润率和大量利润的事实驱动的，但国家行动者的行动是由他们必须确保资本主义关系的再生产并帮助资本家累积价值的事实驱动的。然而，在这些限制之内，国家行动者也有自己的利益要追求。这些包括事业主义，通过与资本家和大土地所有者的合作，在选举中获胜，或从普通民众那里获得对他们统治的某种形式的合法性认可等方式以非经济方式积累财富。马克思（1843）写道，“就单个官僚而言，国家的终极目标成为他的私人目标：追求更高的职位，建立职业生涯。”国家行动者追求自己的利益，因为这样做对他们有益。他们之所以能追求自己的利益，是因为国家的结构给了他们一定的自主性。国家官僚可能寻求“以牺牲他人为代价为自己谋取利益”（Harman, 1991），即资本家和其他群体。当然，他们能在这个方向上走多远取决于资本家（和其他人）如何行使他们的能动性。作为对可能伤害资本家的国家行动者行动的回应，资本家可能“利用他们自己的特殊位置——工业资本可以延迟投资，金融资本可以移至海外——进行反击”（Harman, 1991）。
在追求自己的利益时，国家行动者可能会做一些并不完全符合资本家短期利益的事情，事实上，他们可以追求自己相对独立于资本家利益的利益。在限制之内，自主行动的国家行动者与拥有最终经济否决权的资本家之间可以存在冲突。某些行动者可能在对待群众方面有点改革性和/或可能追求自己的利益，因此可能实施被视为资本家利润的消耗的政策。国家行动者对促进资本积累所需的看法可能与资本主义阶级及其各个部分的客观利益不完全一致。
国家行动者，尤其是政治家，可以被视为涉及政治中的M-C-M'-式的循环，其中政治家投资金钱（M）贿赂穷人选民和反对党代表以及政治宣传和政治暴力（例如，对对手的身体强制）以制造政治商品，称为政府权力I，然后使用它作为工具来赚取比投资更多的钱（M'）。同样，国家官员（尤其是高层官员）投资金钱（以贿赂政治家和/或其他官员的形式）占据可以产生大量租金（贿赂）的职位。因此，他们也参与了M-C-M'-式的循环。如果资本家及其政治代表以及国家官僚投资的钱比花费的多，那么这些钱最终来自哪里：这最终来自工人/选民没有获得满足他们需要的钱。
国家行动者自私行为的限度；这些行动不能太多也不能太久地破坏积累条件。这些限制由资本主义作为一种关系结构对国家作为另一种关系结构设定的。积累提供就业，否则可能会出现特定政权乃至国家合法性的危机。积累是国家自身作为一个资产阶级国家获得税收和贷款的来源。“指导层在国家官僚机构中依赖于成功的资本家剥削和积累”，如果没有这个，他们就无法获得所需的收入，包括“为了其自身的特权和其自身的运作”（Harman, 1991）。因此，国家行动者被迫提供促进资本积累的条件，这些条件在国家的地理边界内。Mandel（1969）说，资本主义阶级“在很大程度上填满了国家的金库”，因此支付了“国家的维护费用”，这意味着它“将要求后者通过其法律和机构完全为他们服务”。

请将“Of course, I would argue, this financial reliance of the state is derivative and conjunctural. Institutional separation — of the state from the profit-oriented market economy (i.e. the fact that state institutions are not the headquarters of big companies) — does not mean processual separation between the state and the private sector. What the state does or does not do, either in the interest of a country as a whole (to the extent that economic growth benefits both capitalists and workers) or in the interests of the masses is fundamentally driven by state’s class character — that is, its need to support economic and political interests of the exploiting class — and not primarily because of its own dependence on capitalists. Such dependence does exist. It exists at a more concrete level. And it does have important effects on capitalists and non-capitalist classes.
4	Limits to state function in support of capitalist interests
The state can create general conditions for capitalist development by connecting to every step in the M-C-M' circuit, as we have seen in Chapter 7. Conversely, its actions, intentionally or not, can impede the development of productive forces under capitalism. ‘The state power has been able . . . to cooperate with capitalist development.’16 Or it can ‘put brakes on it’ (Trotsky, 1991: 212).
While the state promotes capitalist accumulation in various ways, there are limits to the extent to which the state succeeds and can succeed in doing so. These limits are given in the concrete mechanisms creating conditions for capitalist accumulation. This is for various reasons.
Firstly, given its derivative dependence on the capitalist class for its survival (tax dependence), the state has limited resources in its hands at any given point in time, and this limits what state actors can do for capitalists (and in their own interests). The state can help capitalists — for example, it can help a relatively weak enterprise under its jurisdiction to close the gap between its own value and the social value — but there are strong limits to any given state doing this because the state is subordinated to the law of value operating at the global scale, the law that ultimately determines how much value (or, money) will pass through the state.
Secondly, since the state is ‘separate’ from the sphere of production, it only reacts to accumulation crisis.17 Besides, specific policies may not be adequate to capitalist accumulation (Jessop, 2012: 340; Jessop, 2006: 418) because within structural limits (e.g. the limit to protect property rights, commodity relations, and so on), ‘there will always be more than one possible ‘accumulation strategy’ (Jessop, 1990: 159). The different capitalists may have different interests and different ideas about their interests, and they might contradict each other. Accumulation strategies must advance the immediate interests of the different fractions of capital (located in different territories) and must secure the longterm interests of the hegemonic fraction, at least, which must, in turn, sacrifice some of its short-term economic interests. It is not an easy task to figure out what these will be in a context where different fractions of capital have different interests and where capitalists and workers are antagonistically related. No capitalist fraction knows exactly what policies are needed at the level of the whole economy, especially if the economy is constantly crisis-prone. Trial and error is one of the methods in which specific pro-business policies are arrived at. But the bureaucratic state is not always equipped to innovatively think about new actions that are needed as it is best designed to follow fixed legal structures in processing certain inputs (Offe, 1984).
Thirdly, a given intervention can be contradictory: certain positive impacts of an action might be nullified by its negative impacts. Some people think that military expenditures are good for capitalist accumulation. Luxemburg (1913) says: militarism is a ‘pre-eminent means for the realisation of surplus-value; it is in itself a province of accumulation’. For Harvey (2008b), the importance of the wars is that value is destroyed creating opportunities for the production of new value. From a class fractional approach (from the standpoint of certain capitalists), this might sound reasonable: the favourable impact of militarism on capitalist accumulation ‘is how things may appear from the standpoint of individual capital as military supplies have always been the occasion for rapid enrichment’ (Grossman, 1929). But from the standpoint of capital as a whole, ‘militarism is a sphere of unproductive consumption’ in the sense that ‘[i]nstead of being saved, values are pulverised’. This means that ‘[f]ar from being a sphere of accumulation, militarism slows down accumulation’ as ‘a major share of the income of the working class’ which the state secures through indirect taxation and which ‘might have gone into the hands of the capitalists as surplus value is seized by the state and spent mainly for unproductive purposes’ (ibid.).
Similarly, if the state reduces/eliminates support for the unemployed (unem-ployment insurance), it may reduce its expenses to the delight of capitalists who are always for austerity, and it may reduce the wages by weakening the bargaining power of workers, but without the state support, unemployed people may not be available in a place for capitalists to take advantage of them. The reserve army must be in situ. To cite another instance, if the state invests more in support of capital it is good for capital, but increased state borrowing may raise interest rate which adversely affects capital (and not just capital) (Resnick and Wolff, 2006: 319).
Fourthly, the more intense demands on the state can threaten to overburden it. Policies of the state can pose a threat to the dominance of the capitalist exchange relations. The more the state is involved in the economy, the less the investment opportunities for certain segments of capital there are, and the greater is the proportion of the social surplus product that it absorbs, which could otherwise be in the hands of the capitalist class.18 While the economic purpose — maintaining conditions for capitalist economic development — is a prime motive of the state, the state does engage in various non-economic activities that can undermine conditions for capitalist development.
Fifthly, the capitalist state cannot be solely concerned with benefitting the capitalist class. A hegemonic class must ensure that there is a space within which the state meets the interests of non-capitalist classes and class fractions, including large-scale landowners, and top layers of state actors. To be the capitalist state, the state does not have to be exclusively concerned with the capitalist class. In fact, the state has to relate to all the classes, to the totality of capitalist class society. The state seeks to meet the needs of non-capitalist classes from time to time, in howsoever limited a manner; it must meet the common socialecological needs of all classes (laws of marriage, traffic lights, pandemic, global warming), even if the ways in which it meets the common needs are shaped by its capitalist character. To the extent that the interests of the capitalist class are fundamentally incompatible with those of the workers (as well as small-scale producers), the fact that the state must meet the needs of the non-capitalist classes puts a limit to the extent to which the state can meet the needs of the capitalist class at a point in time, even if welfare policies create a market for capitalists. After all, every deduction from the surplus value that does not go to the capitalist class is considered to be a loss to that class, other things constant.
Sixthly, the capitalist class needs to make money, and for this to happen certain cultural-political conditions need to be created, and these conditions may not be necessarily conducive to profit-making in any direct sense. If to divert attention from the failure of capitalists and their state to better the working and living conditions of the masses, the state run by a right-wing party supported by its own bigoted supporters decides to spend its energy on cultural and sentimental matters, then creating general conditions for capitalist accumulation may be compromised. In India, the state under a right-wing fascistic party is indeed often more concerned about promoting the religious nationalist agenda than creating general conditions for capitalist development. Such an agenda involves strategies and interventions that are more or less useless from the standpoint of the common affairs of the capitalist class.
Seventhly, the ultimate limit is the tendency for the rate of profit to fall as the organic composition of capital rises. As there is less surplus value to play with, the state’s ability to intervene (including on behalf of labour) is diminished. The recurrent economic crisis, over which the state has little control, causes unemployment. And ‘[g]rowing unemployment, in its turn, deepens the financial crisis of the state and undermines the unstable monetary systems’ (Trotsky, 1938a).
5	Summary, and theoretical and political implications
It is partly because of the capitalist structure that capitalists need to exercise their agency by making use of instrumentalist connection to state actors: the structural imperative on the state to do certain things has no guarantee that a given policy will be adequate and will always happen on time. Capitalists have their associations and organizations (including chambers of commerce, bourgeois parties, and so on) through which their class agency is exercised and through which they launch class struggle (blocking pro-people measures and repressing people’s struggles). Panitch and Gindin (2015: 9) say that capitalist institutions ‘like employers’ associations, confederations of industry, chambers of commerce, think tanks, consultancies, law firms, foundations and philanthropies mediate and fashion ‘capital’s collective class interests’. Of course, the state itself is the most trustworthy organization of the capitalist class (if not a specific regional or social fraction of the class). Instrumentalism is an important mechanism, in other words.
It is also the capitalist structure that leaves a space for the autonomy of the state as a structure, and its autonomy is enjoyed by state actors. Their autonomy is a double-edged sword. It allows the state (actors) to manage the common affairs of the various fractions of the capitalist class (apart from the interests of society as a whole), and yet state actors can pursue their own interests.
Views of state actors, including how they see accumulation in relation to the ‘national interest’, are important. Their own interests in accumulation of power and wealth (and their own interests may not always coincide with those of capitalist class as a whole) might influence how (well) the state promotes capitalist economic interests. The struggles of the masses have an impact on the thinking and interventions of the state as well: sometimes the state might grant concessions, and at other times, it might not. Some state actors, like some capitalists, can be compassionate and philanthropic, but qua state actors, they cannot do much. State actors may not always succeed in ensuring conditions for capitalist profit-making, and not every state action is pro-capitalist, but that does not mean that the state is not inherently capitalist. Just because a piece of art produced by a poor artist living in a remote village may be sold for a million, that does not invalidate the law of value.”翻译为中文

当然，我会争论，国家对资本阶级的财政依赖是衍生性的和情景性的。机构的分离——即国家从以利润为导向的市场经济中分离出来（即国家机构不是大公司的总部）——并不意味着国家与私营部门之间在过程上的分离。国家做或不做的事情，无论是出于整个国家的利益（在经济增长惠及资本家和工人的程度上）还是出于大众的利益，都是由国家的阶级性质根本驱动的——也就是说，它需要支持剥削阶级的经济和政治利益——而不主要是因为它自己对资本家的依赖。这种依赖确实存在。它存在于更具体的层面上。并且对资本家和非资本家阶级确实有重要影响。

4 支持资本主义利益的国家功能的局限性
通过连接到M-C-M'循环的每个步骤，如我们在第7章中看到的那样，国家可以为资本主义发展创造一般条件。相反，其行动，无论是否有意，都可以阻碍资本主义下生产力的发展。“国家权力能够......与资本主义发展合作。”16 或者它可以“给它制动”（托洛茨基，1991：212）。

虽然国家以各种方式促进资本主义积累，但就国家成功和能够成功的程度而言存在局限性。这些局限性存在于为资本主义积累创造条件的具体机制中。这是由于各种原因。

首先，由于国家对资本主义阶级的生存（税收依赖性）有衍生性依赖，国家在任何给定的时间点上手中有限的资源，这限制了国家行为者可以为资本家（和他们自己的利益）做的事情。国家可以帮助资本家——例如，它可以帮助其管辖范围内相对较弱的企业缩小自身价值与社会价值之间的差距——但任何给定国家做这些事情的能力都存在强烈的限制，因为国家受到全球范围内运作的价值规律的支配，这一规律最终决定了将有多少价值（或者，金钱）通过国家。

其次，由于国家与生产领域“分离”，它只对积累危机作出反应。17 此外，特定政策可能对资本主义积累不足(Jessop, 2012: 340; Jessop, 2006: 418)，因为在结构性限制内（例如保护财产权、商品关系等限制），"总会有不止一种可能的‘积累策略’"(Jessop, 1990: 159)。不同的资本家可能有不同的利益和对他们利益的不同看法，他们可能相互矛盾。积累策略必须推进不同资本部分（位于不同领域）的即时利益，并且必须确保至少霸权部分的长期利益，这反过来必须牺牲一些短期经济利益。在不同资本部分有不同利益，资本家和工人处于对立关系的情况下弄清楚这些将是什么并不容易。没有一个资本部分确切地知道整体经济层面需要什么政策，特别是如果经济持续易于危机。试错法是得出特定亲商政策的方法之一。但官僚国家并不总是装备得当，以创新的方式思考所需的新行动，它最擅长遵循固定的法律结构来处理某些输入(Offe, 1984)。

第三，给定的干预可能是矛盾的：一个行动的某些积极影响可能被其负面影响抵消。有些人认为军事支出对资本主义积累有好处。卢森堡（1913）说：军国主义是“实现剩余价值的一个杰出手段；它本身就是积累的一个省份”。对于哈维（2008b)来说，战争的重要性在于价值被摧毁，为生产新价值创造了机会。从阶级分割的角度看（从某些资本家的立场来看），这可能听起来合理：军国主义对资本主义积累的有利影响“是从个别资本的立场看时的情况，因为军事供应一直是迅速致富的机会”（格罗斯曼，1929）。但从整体资本的立场来看，“军国主义是非生产性消费的领域”，意味着“价值不是被保存，而是被粉碎”。这意味着“军国主义远非是积累的领域，它减缓了积累”的速度，因为“工人阶级的主要收入部分”（国家通过间接税收获得，而“本可以进入资本家手中成为剩余价值的部分被国家没收并主要用于非生产性目的”（同上）。

类似地，如果国家减少/取消对失业者的支持（失业保险），它可能会减少其支出，让总是支持紧缩政策的资本家感到高兴，同时通过削弱工人的议价能力降低工资，但没有国家支持，失业者可能无法在资本家需要利用他们的地方可用。后备军必须在场。再举一个例子，如果国家更多地支持资本投资，对资本是有利的，但增加的国家借贷可能会提高利率，这对资本（不仅仅是资本）产生不利影响（Resnick 和 Wolff, 2006: 319）。

第四，对国家的更加强烈的需求可能会威胁到国家的过度负担。国家的政策可能会对资本主义交换关系的支配地位构成威胁。国家在经济中的参与越多，某些资本部分的投资机会就越少，它吸收的社会剩余产品的比例就越大，这些本可以掌握在资本家阶级手中。18 虽然经济目的——维持资本主义经济发展的条件——是国家的主要动机，但国家确实从事各种非经济活动，这些活动可能会破坏资本主义发展的条件。

第五，资本主义国家不能仅仅关注给资本主义阶级带来好处。一个霸权阶级必须确保有一个空间，国家在其中满足非资本主义阶级和阶级部分的利益，包括大地主，以及国家行动者的高层。要成为资本主义国家，国家不必专门关注资本主义阶级。实际上，国家必须与所有阶级，整个资本主义阶级社会建立关系。国家不时以有限的方式满足非资本主义阶级的需求；它必须满足所有阶级的共同社会生态需求（婚姻法律、红绿灯、大流行、全球变暖），即使满足共同需求的方式受到其资本主义性质的影响。在资本主义阶级的利益与工人（以及小规模生产者）的利益根本不相容的程度上，国家必须满足非资本主义阶级需求的事实限制了国家在某个时间点能够满足资本主义阶级需求的程度，即使福利政策为资本家创造了市场。毕竟，每一次从剩余价值中扣除的不流向资本家阶级的部分，在其他条件不变的情况下，都被认为是对那个阶级的损失。

第六，资本主义阶级需要赚钱，为此需要创造某些文化政治条件，这些条件可能不一定直接有利于盈利。如果为了转移公众对资本家及其国家改善大众工作和生活条件失败的注意力，由一个右翼政党管理的国家得到其偏执支持者的支持，决定将精力花在文化和情感问题上，那么为资本主义积累创造一般条件可能会受到损害。在印度，一个右翼法西斯主义政党领导下的国家确实通常更关心推广宗教民族主义议程，而不是为资本主义发展创造一般条件。这样的议程涉及的策略和干预措施，从资本主义阶级的共同事务的角度看，或多或少是无用的。

第七，最终的限制是随着资本有机构成的上升，利润率倾向于下降。由于可支配的剩余价值较少，国家的干预能力（包括代表劳动力的干预）减弱。反复出现的经济危机，国家几乎无法控制，导致失业。并且“增加的失业，反过来，加深了国家的财政危机并破坏了不稳定的货币体系”（托洛茨基，1938a）。

5 摘要，以及理论和政治含义
部分原因在于资本主义结构，资本家需要通过与国家行动者的工具性联系行使他们的能动性：国家做某些事情的结构性命令没有保证，一个给定的政策将是充分的并且总是及时发生。资本家通过其协会和组织（包括商会、资产阶级政党等）行使其阶级能动性，并通过这些途径发起阶级斗争（阻挠亲民众措施和压制人民斗争）。潘尼奇和金丁（2015：9）说，资本家机构“像雇主协会、行业联合会、商会、智库、咨询公司、律师事务所、基金会和慈善机构调解并塑造‘资本的集体阶级利益’”。当然，国家本身是资本家阶级最值得信赖的组织（如果不是特定地区或社会部分的阶级）。换句话说，工具主义是一个重要机制。

也是资本主义结构为国家作为一个结构的自主性留出了空间，国家行动者享受着这种自主性。他们的自主性是一把双刃剑。它允许国家（行动者）管理资本主义阶级各个部分的共同事务（除了整个社会的利益），但国家行动者可以追求自己的利益。

国家行动者的观点，包括他们如何看待积累与“国家利益”的关系，是重要的。他们在权力和财富积累方面的自身利益（他们的利益并不总是与整个资本主义阶级的利益一致）可能会影响国家促进资本主义经济利益的方式（以及效果）。大众的斗争也会对国家的思考和干预产生影响：有时国家可能会给予让步，有时则不会。有些国家行动者，就像一些资本家一样，可能是富有同情心和慈善心的，但作为国家行动者，他们无法做太多。国家行动者可能不总是成功确保资本主义盈利条件，也并非每一项国家行动都是亲资本主义的，但这并不意味着国家本质上不是资本主义的。仅仅因为一个生活在偏远山村的贫穷艺术家创作的艺术品可能以百万的价格出售，并不使价值规律无效。

Given the active role of bureaucrats in relation to capitalist development, it is indeed easy to think that they are almost like capitalist property owners or that they belong to the same class as capitalist property owners rather than the ones who make sure that capitalists continue to have their property and augment it. In exactly the same way when a dog guards my house it ‘has’ the house ‘as property’, and I hold it only ‘in fee, as possession’ from the dog. 
9	The state and the agency of the working class
Karl Marx (1887: 316) asks in Capital volume 1: ‘What could possibly show better the character of the capitalist mode of production, than the necessity that exists for forcing upon it, by Acts of Parliament’, the simplest of measures in the interest of workers such as the ‘appliances for maintaining cleanliness and health’? Marx gives many reasons for these measures.
One is that capital needs men and women with the suitable physical and mental abilities to work and to produce surplus value, so the state steps in to ensure this. If this is the case, then why is it that so many people live with their very basic needs, including for food and healthcare, remaining unmet? To what extent can the state actually meet people’s needs? Is capital’s need a sufficient reason for state’s pro-worker measures when these occur? What role does the working class play? In this chapter, the focus is on how the state functions to ‘help’ the working class in part as a response to the working-class politics.
Partly by building on the arguments about the state’s role concerning com-modity and property relations in Chapters 4—5, I discuss, in Section 1, the state’s pro-worker interventions and the labour circuit. In Section 2, I turn to the driving forces behind the state’s pro-worker measures. I argue that there is an objective pressure on the state (e.g. general and long-term interests of capital) to intervene on behalf of workers and that this pressure is mediated by working-class struggle. In Section 3, I discuss the limits to what the state can do for workers under capitalism, the limits that exist because of the capitalist control over property and production, nature of capitalist accumulation and its crisis-proneness, and the capitalist state’s bureaucratic functioning and its antiworker politics. In Section 4, I offer general theoretical reflections on the ways in which the state is connected to working-class interests and struggle. Section 5 discusses the practical implications of the ideas discussed in the chapter.”翻译为中文

一方面，认为国家行为者与资本家之间的密切关系对国家的功能影响不大是错误的。另一方面，这种密切关系并不是国家作为结构的本质。首先必须有国家，以便资本家通过与管理国家事务的人建立密切关系来利用它。鉴于资本主义结构，国家结构必须执行某些功能。这在相对抽象的层面上是正确的。然而，在具体层面上，资本家与国家行为者之间的个人和机构联系是现实，国家行为者为了自己的利益而采取的自主行动也是如此。根据他们自己的经验，大众意识到连接资本家与国家的“成千上万的纽带”。

资本家与国家行为者之间存在密切关系以及顶级国家行为者和资本家可能有相似的生活标准，这使一些人认为他们是同一阶级，或者他们属于一个阶级。例如，戴维森（2016: 213）说，国家管理者的收入，“最终源自工人阶级产生的总剩余价值，就像不同类型的私人资本家所获得的利润、利息和租金一样”。资本家的利润以及“这些官员所享受的相对较高水平的报酬、安全性和声望依赖于继续剥削雇佣劳动”这一事实意味着“国家管理者和资本家的利益是相同的”（同上）。

在最根本的层面上，资本家和国家管理者之间的共同利益源于他们的共同阶级地位。他们都是资产阶级的一部分：“就像英国本土公务员系统中的部门常任秘书，和主要公司的首席执行官一样。
（戴维森，2016: 212；加了斜体）
戴维森邀请我们“将资产阶级想象为一系列同心圆”，其中“资本家阶级本身（实际的所有者和资本控制者）占据中心，其他一系列层次向外辐射”（第213页）。他说，“靠近边缘”的群体“与核心资本主义经济活动（生产等）的直接联系逐渐减少”，并且更多地涉及“意识形态、行政或技术”任务，但这些任务“对于资本主义的再生产来说，然而是必要的”（第213页）。这种观点，尽管听起来超级激进，却危险地接近一种权力观社会，其中资本是一种权力形式，国家是另一种权力形式，即使两者被说成是紧密相连的。

克里斯·哈曼（1991）也有类似的观点：
[国家收入]可与流向不同资本部分的其他收入相比较......就像不同资本部分之间因这些不同收入的大小而持续冲突一样，国家官僚和其他资本主义阶级之间也因其从总剩余价值中分得的份额大小而持续冲突。
（加了斜体）
即使在非经济方面，“国家管理者和资本家的利益并无不同”，这是因为“在学校、大学和俱乐部等机构中的共同背景有助于巩固表达……[这两个群体]利益的阶级意识”（戴维森，2016: 213）。这里的假设是，一群人的意识形态位置决定了他们的阶级地位。

类似地，为了“在划分和重新划分阶级线条方面提供一定的灵活性”，并且“为了捕捉日本资本主义的独特性”，奥尔曼（2003: 199）发现“将资本主义阶级的概念扩展到包括高级国家官僚和执政党的主要政治家是必要的”（第199页）。在日本：
一些资本家在形式上是国家机构工作，另一些在形式上是私有机构工作，尽管......大多数这个阶级的主要成员在两者之间分配他们的生活。重要的是，他们都作为资本的化身，以其当前职位允许的任何方式服务其（因此，他们自己）的最佳利益。他们都致力于扩大剩余价值，并在这种情况下受益，尽管当他们是官僚和政治家时这不是立即显而易见的。
（奥尔曼，2003: 199）
[对于大多数主要成员而言，成为一个资本家的过程是从进入国家官僚机构开始的。认识到日本的高级官僚属于资本家阶级，并不意味着我们不能再将他们区分为目前在国家官僚机构中工作的那部分阶级。
（同上）]19
奥尔曼说，有必要为日本的特殊情况建立一个新的“类别。那个类别是‘集体资本家’......正是集体资本家，在官僚、商业和政府职能之间分配他们的时间”（第200页）。
这里绘制的错误类比是基于一个脱离其基本客观特征的资本的主观定义，在资本家和有力的亲资本主义官员之间。资本家之所以是资本家，是因为一种客观关系，而不仅仅是因为他们希望赚钱。官僚机构确实可以非常积极和独立地促进资本家利益，但首先必须有一个国家才有国家官僚机构，而国家的存在是因为有对立的阶级，而官僚机构本身并不是其中之一。将资本家与亲资本主义国家行为者混为一谈是一个错误，就像将后革命过渡社会的官僚机构视为资本主义阶级一样错误。
资本家是指那些掌握大量交换价值、对私有财产和生产的控制权，并且挪用剩余价值，以及国家的强制力量主要用于其利益的人。工人缺乏这些特征。这是阶级关系，正是作为阶级关系的资本主义解释了资本主义国家作为一种关系结构的本质。而国家作为一种关系结构的本质解释了国家行为者的行动，那些管理国家事务的人代表资本家阶级（和大规模地主）的共同利益行事。托洛茨基（1933）说：
对于马克思主义者来说，阶级......有一个科学上的限定意义。阶级的定义不仅仅是通过其在国民收入分配中的参与，而是通过其在经济总体结构中的独立角色以及其在社会经济基础中的独立根源来定义的。
“官僚机构缺乏阶级的所有这些社会特征”（同上）。 “它在生产和分配过程中没有独立地位。它没有独立的财产根基。其功能主要与阶级统治的政治技术有关”，它“从任何作为‘阶级’特有的特殊财产关系中获得其特权”（同上）。当然，官僚机构可以是剥削性的。但“就官僚机构掠夺人民而言……我们处理的不是科学意义上的阶级剥削，而是社会寄生，尽管规模非常大”（同上）。这与当今的神职人员相似：
在中世纪，神职人员构成了一个阶级或一个阶层，因为其统治依赖于特定的土地财产制度和强迫劳动。当今教会不构成一个剥削阶级，而是一个寄生性团体。实际上将美国的神职人员称为一个特殊的统治阶级是愚蠢的；然而，毫无疑问，不同颜色和宗派的牧师在美国消耗了大量的剩余价值。在寄生性上，官僚机构和神职人员与流氓无产者相似，后者众所周知，同样不代表一个独立的‘阶级’。
（同上）
鉴于官僚在与资本主义发展相关的积极角色，的确很容易认为他们几乎像资本家财产所有者，或者他们属于与资本家财产所有者相同的阶级，而不是确保资本家继续拥有并增加他们的财产的那些人。马克思和恩格斯（1845）说，既然“资产阶级已经在国家中组织了保卫自己财产的防御”，很容易相信“国家拥有工厂作为财产，制造商只是作为占有物持有它”。他们嘲讽地反驳：
就像当一只狗守卫我的房子时，它‘拥有’房子‘作为财产’，我只是从狗那里‘作为占有物持有’房子一样。
9 国家与工人阶级的能动性
卡尔·马克思（1887：316）在《资本论》第一卷中问道：“有什么能比通过议会法案强加于它的最简单的措施——例如为维持清洁和健康的设施——更能表明资本主义生产方式的特征呢？”马克思给出了许多原因。
其中一个原因是，资本需要具有适当的身体和心理能力的男人和女人来工作并产生剩余价值，所以国家介入以确保这一点。如果是这样，为什么还有这么多人的基本需求，包括食物和医疗保健，仍然得不到满足呢？国家实际上能在多大程度上满足人们的需求？当这些发生时，资本的需求是否足以成为国家亲工人措施的充分理由？工人阶级扮演什么角色？本章重点是国家如何部分作为对工人阶级政治的回应来“帮助”工人阶级。
部分基于第4-5章关于国家关于商品和财产关系角色的论证，我在第1节中讨论了国家亲工人的干预和劳动循环。在第2节，我转向国家亲工人措施背后的驱动力。我认为国家存在客观压力（例如，资本的一般和长期利益）代表工人干预，这种压力通过工人阶级斗争得到调解。在第3节，我讨论了国家在资本主义下能为工人做什么的局限性，这些局限性存在于资本家对财产和生产的控制、资本主义积累及其危机倾向性、资本主义国家的官僚机构运作及其反工人政治的性质。在第4节中，我就国家与工人阶级利益和斗争的联系方式提供了一般理论反思。第5节讨论了本章讨论的观点的实践含义。

请将“1	State’s pro-worker interventions and the labour circuit (C-M-C'-R-C)
As suggested in Chapter 4, the state connects to each phase of what I have called the labour circuit (C-M-C'-R-C). The state is, to some extent, involved in the determination of the value of the commodity labour power (C). It influences whether, how and to what extent labour power can be sold through its policies concerning, for example, the rate of employment and the freedom of labour in the labour market. It is involved in the determination of the monetary value of labour power, or wage (M), via the minimum wage policy, and it also supplements the private wage with a social wage. The state influences how much food and other things (C') people can buy with the money wage that they receive, because the state has an impact on inflation through its monetary policy. It also has an impact on the sphere of reproduction (R) through its policies concerning, say childcare or family relations, which contribute to the (re)production of the commodity labour power (C). The concept of the labour circuit can now be expanded in order to shed light on state’s pro-worker interventions, particularly, in the labour-market and the workplace. I will also briefly discuss state’s policies in favour of small-scale producers.
The state fixes ‘[Reasonable wages’ and ‘the limits of the working-day’ (Marx, 1887: 182) apart from various other measures. The minimum wage legislation, if properly implemented, can benefit workers who receive very low wages.1 If the mandated minimum wage is significantly above the market rate in many sectors, the minimum wage legislation could have a positive impact on the determination of the value of labour power as it puts a floor on the wage level and indeed on the actual wages paid. A lower minimum wage would have the opposite effect.
In part because workers do not receive the cost of production of labour power from private employers, millions are in poverty who receive some state assistance (e.g. subsidized food), which really amounts to subsidizing capitalist companies who fail to ensure the reproduction of their workforce. The state constructs the poverty line to target welfare provision to the population below the poverty line. The poverty line represents state’s perception of what people absolutely need to live, a perception that is determined by capitalists’ interests. The state’s poverty line, like the state-defined minimum wage, will have an impact on the value of labour power and on its monetary expression. A higher poverty line, if implemented, will put pressure on the buyers of labour power to pay higher wages. This will reduce the extent of the violation of the principle of ‘exchange equivalent for equivalent’ (Marx, 1887: 123).
During the infancy of capitalism, the state makes workers work long hours or supports capitalists making workers work long hours. As capitalism progresses, the state limits the working day (especially when technical change allows employers to extra more labour every hour): ‘The legal limitation of the working-day puts an end to such mischief’ (Marx, 1887: 385).2 Indeed, Factory Acts came into force both in advanced countries and in the periphery.3
There has been a need for the state to make unfree labour illegal, including by acting against regulations characteristic of pre-capitalist societies. State inter-vention has included laws against bonded labour, and laws that restrict child labour, and prohibits child labour below certain age.4 The fact that millions of people, including children, work as forced labour means that the state has not succeeded, thanks to the power of the capitalist buyers of the labour power. The state may ‘make elementary education a compulsory condition to the “productive” employment of children’ (Marx, 1887: 274) under a certain age.5 The state sometimes provides small loans or subsidized necessaries to workers and small-scale producers, but this intervention is inadequate. The state has policies against adulterated food.6 Through its land reforms programme, the state may also weaken the power of rent-receiving landlords because of whom the ground rent exists which in turn directly or indirectly affects the economic conditions (e.g. cost of food) of the workers and small-scale producers. In addition, the state in some countries has ‘tried to’ guarantee that the produce of farmers will be bought at a minimum support price by the government, thus protecting them against the uncertainties in the market demand and market price.
The state seeks to feed the reserve army in situ, including the technologically induced floating population,7 sometimes by helping people set up micro-enterprises, thus ensuring that the unemployed stay where they are just in case they are needed by the big business. It also invests in public works to create some employment and provides tax breaks to companies to attract them to create jobs in poverty-stricken areas (e.g. opportunity zones policy in the US discussed earlier).
All in all, some of the state’s interventions aim to ‘protect the workers’ and can be ‘against the interests of the employers’. There is no doubt that the workers, especially in advanced countries, have enjoyed some benefits from these policies. These legislations tend to weaken some of the worse abuses (or excesses).8 In the sectors and areas/countries that are not regulated, works suffer more, and this suggests that state regulation has made some difference. The working class of the world as a whole is more educated and healthier, and it lives longer now than it did 300 years ago. Of course, there is enormous geographical variation across jurisdictions of nation-states and between socially privileged and socially under-privileged groups inside a country (e.g. African-Americans in the US, ex-untouchables in India, indigenous peoples in Canada).
State intervention in favour of non-capitalist groups — the working-class people (both the employed and under/un-employed) and small-scale producers — can be of different types. One can think about state policies along two dimensions: (a) whether the intervention is primarily at the level of circulation (market-based) or production and (b) whether the intervention is commodified or de-commodified (Wright, 1985: 298). Taking these two dimensions together, we can produce the following four-fold typology of state interventions (as shown in Table 9.1) (ibid.).
To illustrate the differences between these types, state interventions to deal with the problem of malnutrition or food insecurity could be used as an example (Wright, 1985: 299). Food stamps would be a commodified-circulation intervention (CC): the state simply redistributes a targeted income to certain groups to be spent in the open market for the acquisition of food. Free distribution of surplus food to the poor would be a de-commodified-circulation 
intervention (DC). Government subsidies to farmers to encourage them to produce certain food products for poor people which otherwise might not be profitable to produce would be a commodified production intervention (CP). The minimum support price policy falls in this category too. State-run farms to produce food for the poor would be a de-commodified production intervention (DP).
2	Driving forces behind state action in favour of workers
(and small-scale producers)
The question is this: why does the state, the state of capitalists (and large-scale landowners), intervene on behalf of the masses? The answer must be from multiple vantage points, including working-class revolt and the capitalist class society (general, long-term) interests of the capitalist as a whole and various capitalist groups. Also, in thinking about the state, a distinction has to be made between pretext for state action and its cause. A state action in the interest of workers and small-scale producers tends to find its immediate pretext (not its cause) in such things as a society-wide health crisis (e.g. pandemic) that decimates the people making it difficult for capitalists to have access to enough workers at reasonable wages.9
Class struggle
Given the immanent potential for class conflict within the exploitative capitalist system, the capitalist state granting material concessions is an attempt to produce common people’s consent to the system and to subdue the intensity of actual class struggle as well as pre-empt future class struggle. State interventions in relation to working conditions — for example, Parliamentary Acts — are partly a result of class struggle between the two main classes and not ‘the products of Parliamentary fancy’ (Marx, 1887: 187). ‘Their formulation, official recognition, and proclamation by the State, were the result of a long struggle of classes’ (ibid.).
Clearly, ‘[t]he history of this struggle shows two opposed tendencies’ (Marx, 1887: 181). This is in the sense that, for example, during the earliest stage in 
capitalism’s history, and with respect to the working-day, the statutes of the state ‘tried to lengthen it by compulsion’, ‘[w]hilst the modern Factory Acts compulsorily shortened the working-day’ (ibid.). Indeed, ‘in its adult condition’, with workers’ organization getting stronger, they have received ‘the concessions that, growling and struggling, it [i.e. capital] has to make’ (ibid.; italics added). Indeed, acting in its own collective interest, the working class has demanded such things as the reduction in the working day: ‘The establishment of a normal working-day is the result of centuries of struggle between capitalist and labourer’ (ibid.: 181).10 Of course, while class struggle contributes to the introduction of pro-worker measures at sub-national and national scales, the nature and the impacts of the legislations can vary geographically. There were important differences between the measures taken by the French state and those by the English state, for example.11
The laws of commodity exchange impose no limit to how much capitalists exploit workers, and because of the same laws, the state can do little except under ‘compulsion from society’ (Marx, 1887: 181). This compulsion is in the form of labour struggle: ‘[I]n the history of capitalist production, the determination of what is a working day, presents itself as the result of a struggle, a struggle between collective capital, i.e., the class of capitalists, and collective labour, i.e., the working-class’ (Marx, 1887: 164).
In fact, both wages and the working day are a function of the relative balance of power. Marx (1865) writes in Value, price and profit that the upper limit to profit is ‘limited by the physical minimum of wages and the physical maximum of the working day’ and that, therefore, how much profit capitalists make ‘is only settled by the continuous struggle between capital and labour, the capitalist constantly tending to reduce wages to their physical minimum, and to extend the working day to its physical maximum, while the working man constantly presses in the opposite direction. ‘The matter resolves itself into a question of the respective powers of the combatants,’ Marx says.”翻译为中文

1 国家亲工人干预和劳动循环（C-M-C'-R-C）
如第4章所述，国家与我所称的劳动循环（C-M-C'-R-C）的每个阶段相连。国家在一定程度上参与决定商品劳动力（C）的价值。它通过其政策影响劳动力是否、如何以及在多大程度上可以出售，例如，关于就业率和劳动力市场的自由。它参与决定劳动力的货币价值或工资（M），通过最低工资政策，并且还通过社会工资补充私人工资。国家影响人们能用他们收到的货币工资购买多少食物和其他东西（C'），因为国家通过其货币政策对通胀产生影响。它还通过其关于例如儿童保育或家庭关系的政策影响再生产领域（R），这些政策有助于商品劳动力（C）的（再）生产。劳动循环的概念现在可以扩展，以阐明国家亲工人干预，特别是在劳动力市场和工作场所。我还将简要讨论国家支持小规模生产者的政策。

国家确定“[合理工资”和“工作日的限制”（马克思，1887年：182页）以及各种其他措施。如果得到适当实施，最低工资立法可以惠及收入非常低的工人。1 如果规定的最低工资在许多行业显著高于市场率，最低工资立法可能对劳动力价值的决定产生积极影响，因为它为工资水平乃至实际支付的工资设定了底线。最低工资较低将产生相反效果。

部分原因是工人没有从私人雇主那里获得劳动力生产成本，数以百万计的人生活在贫困中，他们得到了一些国家援助（例如，补贴食品），这实际上等于补贴了未能确保其劳动力再生产的资本主义公司。国家构建贫困线，以将福利提供目标对准贫困线以下的人口。贫困线代表国家对人们绝对需要生活的东西的看法，这一看法由资本家利益决定。国家的贫困线，像国家定义的最低工资一样，将对劳动力的价值及其货币表现产生影响。如果实施，更高的贫困线将对劳动力的购买者施压，要求支付更高的工资。这将减少“等价交换”的原则被侵犯的程度（马克思，1887：123）。

在资本主义发展的初期，国家使工人工作长时间或支持资本家使工人工作长时间。随着资本主义的发展，国家限制了工作日（特别是当技术变革允许雇主每小时提取更多劳动时）：“对工作日的法律限制结束了这种恶习”（马克思，1887：385）。2 实际上，工厂法案在发达国家和周边地区都开始实施。3

国家需要使非自由劳动非法，包括通过对抗前资本主义社会特有的规定的行动。国家干预包括针对契约劳工的法律，限制童工法律，禁止某一年龄以下的童工。4 数百万人，包括儿童，作为强迫劳动而工作，这意味着由于资本家劳动力购买者的力量，国家未能成功。国家可能“将基础教育作为儿童‘生产性’就业的强制条件”（马克思，1887：274），直到一定年龄。5 国家有时向工人和小规模生产者提供小额贷款或补贴必需品，但这种干预是不足的。国家有反对掺假食品的政策。6 通过其土地改革计划，国家还可能削弱领取地租的地主的力量，这些地主使地租存在，从而直接或间接影响工人和小规模生产者的经济条件（例如，食品成本）。此外，一些国家“试图”保证政府将以最低支持价格购买农民生产的农产品，从而保护他们免受市场需求和市场价格不确定性的影响。
国家寻求就地养活后备军，包括技术引起的流动人口，7 有时通过帮助人们建立微型企业，从而确保失业者留在原地，以防万一大企业需要他们。它还投资于公共工程以创造一些就业，并为公司提供税收减免，以吸引它们在贫困地区创造就业机会（例如，美国之前讨论的机会区政策）。
总的来说，国家的一些干预旨在“保护工人”，可能“与雇主的利益相反”。无疑，特别是在发达国家，工人从这些政策中享受到了一些好处。这些立法倾向于削弱一些更糟糕的滥用行为（或过度行为）。8 在未受监管的部门和地区/国家，工人受苦更多，这表明国家监管确实有所不同。整个世界的工人阶级现在比300年前受过更多教育，更健康，寿命更长。当然，不同国家管辖区之间以及一个国家内社会特权和社会弱势群体之间存在巨大的地理差异（例如，美国的非裔美国人，印度的前贱民，加拿大的土著人）。

国家支持非资本主义群体的干预——工人（包括受雇和失业/未就业者）和小规模生产者——可以有不同类型。可以从两个维度考虑国家政策：（a）干预主要是在流通（基于市场）还是生产层面，（b）干预是商品化还是非商品化（赖特，1985：298）。将这两个维度结合起来，我们可以得出国家干预的以下四分类型（如表9.1所示）（同上）。
以解决营养不良或食物不安全问题的国家干预为例来说明这些类型之间的差异（赖特，1985：299）。食品券将是一种商品化流通干预（CC）：国家只是将目标收入重新分配给某些群体，以便在开放市场上购买食物。向穷人免费分发剩余食物将是一种非商品化流通干预（DC）。政府补贴农民以鼓励他们生产一些可能对穷人有益但不赚钱的食物将是一种商品化生产干预（CP）。最低支持价格政策也属于此类别。国有农场为穷人生产食物将是一种非商品化生产干预（DP）。

2 支持工人（和小规模生产者）的国家行动背后的驱动力
问题是：为什么国家，资本家（和大地主）的国家，要代表大众进行干预？答案必须从多个角度出发，包括工人阶级反抗和资本主义阶级社会（一般，长期）资本家整体和各个资本家集团的利益。同时，在考虑国家时，必须区分国家行动的借口和其原因。国家对工人和小规模生产者的利益进行的行动往往在其直接借口（而不是其原因）中找到，比如社会范围的健康危机（例如，大流行）消灭了人们，使资本家难以以合理的工资获得足够的工人。9

阶级斗争
鉴于剥削性资本主义体系内固有的阶级冲突潜力，资本主义国家给予物质让步是为了获得普通人对体系的同意，并减轻实际阶级斗争的激烈程度以及预防未来的阶级斗争。与工作条件有关的国家干预——例如，议会法案——部分是两个主要阶级之间阶级斗争的结果，而不是“议会幻想的产物”（马克思，1887：187）。 “它们的制定、官方认可和国家的宣告，是长期阶级斗争的结果”（同上）。

显然，“这场斗争的历史显示了两种对立的趋势”（马克思，1887：181）。这意味着，例如，在资本主义历史的最早阶段，就工作日而言，国家的法规“试图通过强制来延长它”，“[而]现代工厂法案强制缩短了工作日”（同上）。实际上，“在成熟状态下”，随着工人组织变得更强大，他们收到了“咆哮和挣扎中必须做出的让步”（同上；加了斜体）。实际上，为了自己的集体利益，工人阶级要求诸如减少工作日之类的事情：“建立正常工作日是几个世纪以来资本家和劳工之间斗争的结果”（同上：181）。10 当然，虽然阶级斗争有助于在次国家和国家规模引入亲工人措施，但立法的性质和影响在地理上可以有所不同。例如，法国国家和英国国家采取的措施之间存在重要差异。11

商品交换法则对资本家剥削工人的程度没有限制，因为同样的法则，国家除了在“社会的强制下”外几乎无能为力（马克思，1887：181）。这种强制是以劳动斗争的形式出现的：“在资本主义生产的历史中，什么是工作日的决定，呈现为一场斗争的结果，一场集体资本，即，资本家阶级，和集体劳动，即，工人阶级之间的斗争”（马克思，1887：164）。

事实上，工资和工作日都是相对力量平衡的函数。马克思（1865）在《价值、价格和利润》中写道，利润的上限“受到工资的物理最低限度和工作日的物理最高限度的限制”，因此，资本家能赚多少利润“只能通过资本和劳动之间的持续斗争来解决，资本家不断倾向于将工资降至其物理最低限度，并将工作日延长至其物理最高限度，而工人则不断朝相反方向施压。”“这个问题归结为双方的相对力量，”马克思说。

请将“It is not just that the state grants concessions (e.g. shorter working days) because of the workers’ struggle. In fact, because of the political pressure from below, the state grants to the workers their unionization right itself, and unionization in turn puts pressure on the state (and capital): ‘only against its will and under the pressure of the masses’ did the British state (in the form of the parliament) ‘give up the laws against Strikes and Trades’ Unions, after it had itself, for 500 years, held, with shameless egoism, the position of a permanent Trades’ Union of the capitalists against the labourers’ (Marx, 1887: 525). The struggle to establish unions, and especially the unions that are independent of the enterprise management, continues as the recent history of India’s Maruti workers struggle shows (White, J., 2017).
While concessions are promised and granted by the state due to reformoriented struggles, the connection between the two — concessions and struggles — is stronger, however, if/when there is a threat to capitalist property interests (a threat to public order) from people’s struggles.12 In the US context, historically, according to Piven and Cloward (1974: 147):
relief arrangements are initiated or expanded during the occasional outbreaks of civil disorder produced by mass unemployment, and are then abolished or contracted when political stability is restored. . . . [E]expan- sive relief policies are designed to mute civil disorder, and restrictive ones to reinforce work norms. In other words, relief policies are cyclical-liberal or restrictive depending on the problems of regulation in the larger society with which government must contend.
While the capitalists and the state do not grant anything without a struggle by the masses, it is also the case that capitalism creates conditions for class struggle. It does this by promoting transportation and communication technologies and removing geographical barriers to working-class unity through the spatial con-centration of workers in specific localities and cities, all in its own economic interest (easy access to a labour pool). Capitalism — ‘the direct and open sway of capital’ — also removes non- or pre-capitalist social relations ‘behind which the dominion of capital is still in part concealed’. With the ‘destruction of petty and domestic industries’, gone is ‘the sole remaining safety-valve of the whole social mechanism’, and this in turn ‘generalises the direct opposition’ of the masses to capital (Marx, 1887: 329). In fact, as non-capitalist ways of reproduction (e.g. family-based production, access to common property resources) vanish, workers increasingly depend on the state to meet the gap between what they need and what they receive from employers. And since the state working on behalf of capital will not give anything automatically, that is, without a struggle, people fight for concessions.
Capital’s interests (coinciding with workers’ interests)
There is more to the story of pro-worker measures than just working-class struggle forcing the state to intervene on behalf of labour. Behind the proworker legislations, there are often long-term capitalist interests: ‘Apart from the working-class movement that daily grew more threatening, the limiting of factory labour [by the state] was dictated by the same necessity which spread guano over the English fields.’ In other words, ‘[t]he same blind eagerness for plunder that in the one case exhausted the soil, had, in the other, torn up by the roots the living force of the nation’ (Marx, 1887: 166).
Capital engages in the normal process of exploitation when workers surrender to capital a large part of the net product. And then there is excessive (= above-normal) exploitation, causing the degeneration of the working population. The excesses (abuses) inflicted on workers are a product of the mutually competing capitalists when they lower the wages below the average cost of maintenance of workers, make workers work excessively long hours of work, cut down on spending for workplace safety (e.g. protective equipment during a pandemic, enough working space per worker, safety of chemicals), and so on. ‘Free competition brings out the inherent laws of capitalist production, in the shape of external coercive laws having power over every individual capitalist’ (Marx, 1887: 181). These external laws of capitalist production meet with the extra-economic laws: ‘Capital is reckless of the health or length of life of the labourer, unless under compulsion from society’ (ibid.; italics added). This compulsion from society is represented by pro-worker state interventions against capital’s recklessness of workers’ health and well-being and against the violation of equal exchange in the labour market (p. 181). In general, the measures regulating the working conditions develop ‘gradually out of circumstances as natural laws of the modern mode of production’(Marx, 1887: 187) in the sense that excesses of capitalism (e.g. long working day, low wages) would result in a crippled reproduction of the only source of surplus value (i.e. labour).
Thus, short working day is not entirely due to class struggle from below: long-term interests are important explanations, especially when ‘the living force of the nation’ (Marx, 1887: 166) is endangered including during a society-wide economic crisis and/or ecological crisis (pandemic). It is important to note that
the mere fear of contagious diseases which do not spare even ‘respectability’ [that is, the bourgeoisie and well-off strata close to it] brought into existence from 1847 to 1864 no less than 10 Acts of Parliament on sanitation, and that the frightened bourgeois in some towns . . . took strenuous measures through their municipalities.
(ibid.: 458)
Individual capitalists are cogs in the wheel of competition. Their shortterm interests — capitalists ‘recklessness’ — directly destroy workers’ health (and they cause the destruction of the environment which in turn affects workers’ bodies/health). This fact undermines long-term interests of capitalists, thus prompting the state to intervene. The regulation of the working day is in the interest of capital and not just labour: if due to overwork and adverse working lives (and environmental pollution), people die faster, then they have to be replaced quicker (the value of labour power will thus go up), and this has a cost for the user of this labouring body, that is, the capitalist class.13 The fact that ‘the forces used up have to be replaced at a more rapid rate and the sum of the expenses for the reproduction of labour-power will be greater’ implies that ‘the interest of capital itself points in the direction of a normal working-day’ (Marx, 1887: 179). If in place of ‘the unnatural extension of the working-day’, and the like that Marx talks about, we put environmental damage, the logic mentioned earlier will still hold. Capitalist production leads to unfavourable effects which need state regulation:
Factory legislation, that first conscious and methodical reaction of society against the spontaneously developed form of the process of production, is . . . just as much the necessary product of modern industry as cotton yam, self-actors, and the electric telegraph.
(Marx, 1887: 315)
In other words, the state is prompted by capitalists’ long-term interests to implement certain policies in favour of workers and the environment, curbing capital’s passion for profit. These policies are ultimately in favour of the capitalist class as a whole, more or less; or they are implemented in such a way that the capitalist class as a whole or some dominant sections of the class benefit from them.14 So the general interest of the capitalist class is crucial to the understanding of pro-worker state policies. The adverse conditions under which workers work and live and which cause illnesses and the crisis of bodily reproduction mean that capitalists can find it difficult to obtain enough people at a wage rate at which enough surplus value can be made (p. 181). The regulation of capitalist production is a political act on the part of capitalist society against spontaneous economic laws and shapes those economic processes.
Similarly, to the extent that the state also allows, however reluctantly, a degree of trade union activity, one reason is the pressure of the masses, as mentioned before. Another reason, once again, is the self-interest of capital: trade union activity ‘has expansionary effects on the market and therefore on the profitability of business’ (Cox, 2002: 249). Besides, a slightly reconciliatory section of the bourgeoisie may use trade unions to subordinate the working class (and small-scale producers), through its collaboration with the trade union leaders, to their party and thus to the state. Indeed, it may promote unions to garner votes and thus political legitimacy for pro-capitalist actions. The current Biden-led government in the US is an example.
The state looks after non-capitalist classes when their interests do not go against, or when they match with, the interests of the capitalist class or its dominant fraction. State policies aimed at providing education and healthcare as well as higher minimum wages can, apart from buying peace, create a market for the capitalist class and thus respond to temporary realization problem within specific sectors and areas. Likewise, the state assistance to farmers would also create rural purchasing power to the benefit of the capitalist class.15 The long-term interests in industrial peace and in the market clearance could partly explain why sometimes sections of the capitalist class (‘patriotic billionaires’) might actually advocate, or tolerate, ‘pro-worker’ reforms (including higher taxes on them and use of the taxes for workers’ welfare).
The ruling class as a whole has certain long-term economic and political interests. Then elements within the ruling class have certain specific interests, and these can be mutually conflicting. Conflicts within the ruling class can help the lower-class struggle in certain cases.16 If a section of the capitalist class needs the support of workers in its battle with, say, landowners, capitalists may support pro-worker legislations.17 Also, inter-capitalist competition contributes to the universalization of the application of government regulations. ‘The first birthright of capital is equal exploitation of labour-power by all capitalists’ (Marx, 1887: 320). In addition, there is ‘the cry of the [bigger] capitalists for equality in the conditions of competition, i.e., for equal restrain on all exploitation of labour’ (ibid.).18 Whenever a law (e.g. a law that limits the labour of children to 6 hours) is applicable to certain enterprises and not to other enterprises, manufacturers in the first category complain against this ‘discrimination’.19 Implied here is a general point about capitalism: ‘since capital is by nature a leveller, since it exacts in every sphere of production equality in the conditions of the exploitation of labour, the limitation by law of’ such things as ‘children’s labour, in one branch of industry, becomes the cause of its limitation in others’ (Marx, 1887: 273).
It is the case that ‘there are major differences and disputes’ among the different branches of the state and within each one of them. These differences, more or less, reflect the different capitalist interests as well as different perceptions by state actors of what needs to be done to maintain conditions for capitalist accumulation and for industrial peace. And, ‘subaltern classes can occasionally use this disarray to score minor victories in some of the state’s more distant outposts’ (Ollman, 2003: 203).”翻译为中文

不仅仅是因为工人的斗争，国家才做出让步（例如，缩短工作日）。实际上，由于来自下层的政治压力，国家授予工人组建工会的权利，而工会反过来对国家（和资本）施加压力：“英国国家（以议会的形式）‘放弃了针对罢工和工会的法律’，只是在大众压力下迫不得已采取的行动，而在此之前，它自己500年来一直以厚颜无耻的自私自利，扮演着永久性资本家工会对抗劳动者的角色”（马克思，1887: 525）。建立工会的斗争，尤其是独立于企业管理层的工会的斗争，仍在继续，正如印度马鲁蒂工人近期斗争的历史所示（白，J., 2017）。

尽管国家因改革导向的斗争而允诺并给予让步，但两者之间的联系——让步和斗争——如果/当资本财产利益受到人民斗争的威胁时（公共秩序的威胁），则联系更加紧密。12 在美国的历史背景下，据皮文和克劳德（1974：147）描述：
救济安排是在大规模失业引发偶尔的民间骚乱期间启动或扩大的，然后在政治稳定恢复时被废除或缩减。...扩大的救济政策旨在平息民间骚乱，而限制性政策则用来加强工作规范。换句话说，救济政策是周期性自由或限制性的，这取决于政府必须应对的更大社会中的管理问题。

尽管资本家和国家不会在没有大众斗争的情况下给予任何东西，资本主义也创造了阶级斗争的条件。它通过推广运输和通信技术以及消除对工人阶级团结的地理障碍来实现这一点，通过在特定地区和城市对工人进行空间集中，在其自身的经济利益中（轻松获取劳动力池）。资本主义——“资本的直接和公开统治”——也消除了非资本主义或前资本主义社会关系，“这些社会关系背后部分隐藏着资本的统治”。随着“小规模和家庭工业的破坏”，整个社会机制的唯一剩余的安全阀消失了，这反过来“使大众对资本的直接对立”普遍化（马克思，1887：329）。事实上，随着非资本主义生产方式（例如，基于家庭的生产，对公共财产资源的使用）的消失，工人越来越依赖国家来弥补他们从雇主那里得到的和他们需要的之间的差距。而由于代表资本运作的国家不会自动给予任何东西，即，没有斗争就没有让步，人们为了获得让步而斗争。

资本的利益（与工人的利益相一致）
亲工人措施背后的故事不仅仅是工人阶级的斗争迫使国家代表劳动力进行干预。背后的亲工人立法，往往有长期的资本家利益：“除了日益威胁的工人阶级运动之外，国家对工厂劳动的限制[由国家颁布]也是出于相同的必要性，这种必要性使得鸟粪铺满了英国的田野。”换句话说，“在一个案例中贪婪地掠夺导致土壤耗尽，在另一个案例中，根本撕裂了国家的生命力”（马克思，1887：166）。

当工人向资本投降大部分净产品时，资本进行正常的剥削过程。然后有过度（=高于正常）的剥削，导致工作人口的退化。对工人施加的过度（滥用）是相互竞争的资本家在他们将工资降低到工人维持平均成本以下、使工人过度长时间工作、削减工作场所安全支出（例如，在大流行期间的防护设备、每个工人的工作空间足够、化学品的安全性等）时产生的。“自由竞争展现了资本主义生产的固有规律，以对每个个别资本家都有权力的外部强制性法律的形式出现”（马克思，1887：181）。这些资本主义生产的外部法则与超经济法则相遇：“资本不顾劳动者的健康或寿命，除非受到社会的强制”（同上；加了斜体）。这种来自社会的强制代表了国家对资本对工人健康和福祉的漠不关心以及对劳动市场中等价交换原则的侵犯采取亲工人干预（第181页）。总的来说，监管工作条件的措施作为现代生产方式的自然规律“逐渐从情况中发展出来”（马克思，1887：187），意味着资本主义的过度（例如，长工作日，低工资）会导致唯一剩余价值来源（即劳动力）的生产受损。
因此，短工作日不完全是由于来自下层的阶级斗争：长期利益是重要的解释，尤其是当“国家的生命力”（马克思，1887：166）受到威胁时，包括在全社会范围的经济危机和/或生态危机（大流行）期间。值得注意的是，
即使“连‘体面’[即，资产阶级及其接近的富裕阶层]也不放过”的传染病的恐惧使得从1847年到1864年有不少于10项关于卫生的议会法案出台，而且在一些城镇里，受惊吓的资产阶级通过他们的市政府采取了积极措施。
（同上：458）
个别资本家是竞争之轮的齿轮。他们的短期利益——资本家的“鲁莽”——直接破坏了工人的健康（并且它们导致了环境的破坏，反过来影响工人的身体/健康）。这一事实破坏了资本家的长期利益，从而促使国家介入。工作日的规范符合资本的利益，而不仅仅是劳动的利益：如果由于过度劳动和不利的工作生活（以及环境污染），人们更快死亡，那么他们必须更快被替换（劳动力的价值将会上升），这对于使用这种劳动身体的用户，即资本家阶级来说有成本。13 事实上，“使用的力量必须以更快的速度替换，劳动力再生产的费用总额将更大”表明“资本本身的利益指向了正常工作日的方向”（马克思，1887：179）。如果我们把马克思谈到的“工作日的不自然延长”等替换为环境破坏，前面提到的逻辑仍然成立。资本主义生产导致不利的影响，需要国家的规制：
工厂法案，作为社会对生产过程自发形式的首次有意识和有条理的反应，......和棉纱、自动纺织机和电报一样，是现代工业的必然产物。
（马克思，1887：315）
换句话说，国家受资本家长期利益的驱使，实施了有利于工人和环境的某些政策，遏制资本对利润的热情。这些政策最终有利于整个资本家阶级，或多或少；或者它们以这样一种方式实施——整个资本家阶级或其一些主导部分从中受益。14 因此，资本家阶级的一般利益对理解亲工人的国家政策至关重要。工人的工作和生活条件恶劣，导致疾病和身体再生产危机，意味着资本家可能难以以足够的工资率获得足够的人，从而创造足够的剩余价值（第181页）。资本主义生产的调节是资本主义社会针对自发经济法则的政治行为，塑造那些经济过程。

同样，国家也允许一定程度的工会活动，尽管勉强，其中一个原因是前面提到的大众的压力。另一个原因，再次，是资本的自身利益：工会活动“对市场和因此对企业的盈利能力有扩张作用”（考克斯，2002：249）。此外，资产阶级中较为和解的一部分可能利用工会通过与工会领导人的合作，将工人阶级（和小规模生产者）置于它们的政党之下，从而置于国家之下。事实上，它可能促进工会以获取选票，从而为亲资本主义行动赢得政治合法性。目前的美国拜登领导的政府就是一个例子。
国家照顾非资本主义阶级的利益，当这些利益不与资本主义阶级或其主导部分的利益相冲突，或者当它们与资本家阶级的利益相匹配时。旨在提供教育和医疗保健以及更高的最低工资的国家政策，除了购买和平之外，还可以为资本家阶级创造市场，从而应对特定部门和地区内的暂时实现问题。同样，对农民的国家援助也会为资本家阶级创造农村购买力。15 工业和平和市场清除的长期利益可以部分解释为什么有时资本家阶级的某些部分（“爱国亿万富翁”）实际上可能主张或容忍“亲工人”改革（包括对他们征收更高的税，并将税收用于工人福利）。
作为一个整体的统治阶级有一定的长期经济和政治利益。然后，统治阶级内部的各个元素有特定的利益，这些利益可能相互冲突。统治阶级内部的冲突在某些情况下可以帮助下层阶级的斗争。16 如果资本家阶级的一个部分在与例如地主的斗争中需要工人的支持，资本家可能支持亲工人立法。17 此外，资本家之间的竞争有助于政府法规的普遍应用。“资本的第一个天赋权利是所有资本家对劳动力的平等剥削”（马克思，1887：320）。此外，有“[较大的]资本家呼吁竞争条件的平等，即，对所有剥削的平等限制”（同上）。18 每当一项法律（例如，将儿童劳动限制为6小时的法律）仅适用于某些企业而不适用于其他企业时，第一类别的制造商会抱怨这种“歧视”。19 这里暗示的是关于资本主义的一个普遍观点：“因为资本本质上是平等主义者，因为它在生产的每个领域都要求劳动剥削条件的平等，所以通过法律限制”诸如儿童劳动之类的事情，“在一个产业部门，成为其他部门限制的原因”（马克思，1887：273）。

事实上，“不同的国家机构之间和它们各自内部存在重大分歧和争议”。这些分歧或多或少反映了不同的资本家利益以及国家行为者对为维持资本积累条件和工业和平需要做什么的不同看法。而且，“下层阶级偶尔可以利用这种混乱，在国家的一些较远的前哨站取得小胜利”（奥尔曼，2003：203）。

请将“Apart from capital’s common and mutually conflicting interests, there is also state’s ‘own’ interest behind its pro-worker interventions. If common people live and work under adverse conditions, and if their physical fitness suffers, this is not good for the state from the standpoint of, for example, war-making (the latter, of course, is ultimately in the interest of the capitalist class as well). The capitalist state needs plenty of able-bodied people for the battlefield just as capitalist employers need them for the workplace.20 Pro-poor attitude of some state actors might also contribute to the people getting some concessions, within limits. Of course, it is a moot point whether the concessions are funded by taxes from the people (workers and small-scale producers) or from the surplus appropriated by capitalists (and large-scale landowners).
3	Limits to state’s pro-worker interventions, and why?
What has been said in the last two sections should be seen in terms of a tendency: under certain conditions, the state tends to grant some concessions, but whether in fact it will and, if it will, how adequate are the concessions, are a contingent matter. In fact, what is promised in the form of pro-worker interventions is usually much bigger than what is delivered.21 There is a limit to the extent to which the state can limit the adverse impacts of capitalism on people. Pro-worker policies are in place, but they are often not fully implemented.22
That the state provides only limited benefits can be seen by looking at the social wage. The social wage, or overall net subsidy, is usually positive (but a very small positive amount) in some of the rich countries (Australia, Canada, Germany, Sweden, and United Kingdom),23 while it is generally negative (that is, a net tax) in the US even during the boom years, and ‘this meant that wage and salary earner paid out more in taxes than they received — they helped reduce any existing fiscal deficit’. In contrast, ‘Over the same interval in Sweden, the net social wage was roughly zero, indicating that its generous social welfare expenditures were actually self-financed’ (p. 538).24 A more recent research reached a similar conclusion:
[I]n almost all advanced capitalist countries, the great bulk of [their] social spending (and especially the part which constitutes labor benefits or the gross social wage) necessarily comes from gross labor income, that is, from personal income taxes, social security contributions, and other personal and property taxes that are levied on labor. To the extent that indirect business taxes or consumption taxes are assumed to be levied on labor, they finance and usually exceed the remaining part of the gross social wage even in the advanced capitalist countries with more developed welfare states than the United States. Therefore, if we assume that indirect taxes are partly paid by labor, the net social wage is significantly negative in the United States (-6.0 percent to -7.5 percent of GDP over the entire postwar period) and from modestly negative to close to zero in other countries.
(Maniatis, 2014: 33)
The working class indeed receives only limited benefits from the state, relative to the assistance that the capitalist class does. It is interesting that the share of labour in national income has been decreasing: it has declined in advanced countries, from about 55% in 1970 to about 40% in 2015, and in emerging market and developing economies, from about 50% in mid-1990s to below 40% in 2015 (Roberts, 2017).
Even if the regulation of capitalist excesses inflicted on workers (very low wages, very long hours of work) is in the interest of long-term interests of capitalists, the latter do not still accept this ‘except “under the pressure of a General Act of Parliament”’ (Marx, 1887: 315). When capitalists reluctantly give in, granting such things as ‘the compulsory regulation of the hours of labour’, they do not give in much (ibid.). Pro-worker interventions are only a ‘meagre concession wrung from capital’ (Marx, 1887: 319), and in the case of England’s Factory Act, it was ‘limited to combining elementary education with work in the factory’ (ibid.). Note that this was the case when capitalism was in a progressive phase relative to its current terminal decline stage. The state indeed continues to fail to implement its own legislation. For example, it fails to enforce the freedom of labourers as sellers of labour power: millions of unfree labourers, including children, work in slave-like conditions. The minimum wage policy is not enforced either, and where it is, usually, the government-legislated minimum wage is lower than a wage floor set within collective agreements (Boeri, 2012).25
All in all, to the extent that the state functions in support of long-term interests of capital, such intervention is more likely when (a) people fight against the effect of exploitation and (b) there are conflicts among the ruling-class elements which means that (c) interests of the working class coincide with interests of some elements of the capitalist class. State’s, or state actors’, own interests and ideas also play a role. Yet the state fails to meet the needs of the working men and women in a significant and durable manner.
Capitalist agency
While pro-worker interventions are due to long-term interests of capital and class struggle, the nature and scope of these interventions are also affected by capitalist class response to these interventions. The capitalist class aims to dilute and/or negate the actual impact of pro-workers policies after they are in put in place. They do this in many ways.
In order to preserve their own competitive advantage, some enterprise owners complain that if a given legislation does not apply to all enterprises, it will advantage those that are not covered by it.26 Capitalists also fight welfare leg-islation on the ground that it is against workers, so they mobilize especially the right-wing politicians to make their case.
The right-wing argument is that unemployment arises when laborers put too high a reserve price on their labour [power]. Labourers create unemployment by refusing to work below a certain minimum wage! This typically happens when welfare is too generous! Ergo, the best way to get rid of unemployment is to reduce welfare to zero.27
(Harvey, 2010a: 281)
When a legislation is imposed on capitalists, they fight this, including through courts and political parties, on the ground that it is an ‘interference with the exploiting rights of capital’ (Marx, 1887: 319). Individual capitalists treat their private property rights as sacrosanct and beyond challenge, so mild actions against them are used to produce hyperbolic catastrophizing notion of the spectre of revolution: ‘They [capitalists] denounced the Factory Inspectors as a kind of revolutionary commissioners’ (Marx, 1887: 188).28 The capitalist tradition of perceiving, and making common people perceive, some slightly reform- mindedly liberals (e.g. Nehru, Obama, Biden) as socialists and even as sym-pathizers of Marxism continues. This is the same tradition of socialist/Left fear-mongering that treats the Democratic party of the US as being on the Left. Considering what are blatantly bourgeois views and actions as Marxist, socialist and leftists, which are then presented as a threat to everything a nation stands for, is a great way of pre-empting/avoiding any pro-worker measure.
When laws regulating the capitalists do exist, capitalists can get around these laws or make it difficult for the state to enforce them, given their control over the process of production and exchange. After all, the workplace is the site of capitalists’ private legislation. This is the site of capitalists’ despotic control which is needed partly to counter potential and actual workers’ resistance inside the workplace. Consider how capitalists respond to the legislation restricting the length of the working day, the use of child labour, and so on. Capitalists resort to multiple mechanisms to get around the pro-worker laws.
Firstly, capitalists make changes in the labour process. For example, they increase the speed of work, leading to increased injury to workers. Capital, more or less, goes unpunished by the state.29 They introduce a relay system: individual (child) workers come to work at different points in time in a 24-hour-day, and this makes it difficult for state officials (inspectors) to monitor whether any worker is working longer than the law allows.30
Secondly, a specific form of change in the labour process is resorting to a technological fix (mechanization), which has two adverse effects on workers. One is that it is a weapon against workers’ struggle: machinery replaces workers weakening their bargaining power. In addition, by enabling ‘the excessive addi-tion of women and children to the ranks of the workers, machinery . . . breaks down the resistance which the male operatives in the manufacturing period continued to oppose to the despotism of capital’ (Marx, 1887: 276). Besides, capital uses more efficient machines to squeeze out as much labour during the reduced working day as during the longer one.
Thirdly, capitalists resort to spatial fix or threaten to do so. If laws to restrict capitalists’ freedom to exploit are in place in one country, capitalists threaten to move their capital elsewhere in order to ‘intimidate’ the state and the workers.
Fourthly, capitalists resort to what can be called a temporal fix. The application of the laws that restrict capitalists’ freedom to exploit is staggered. For example, if the state seeks to raise the minimum wages, the process of increase happens over a long period of time. In the US, the Raise the Wage Act proposed to raise the federal minimum wage to $15 per hour from the current $7.25 in stages: 2021: $9.50; 2022: $11; 2023: 12.50; 2024: 14; 2025: $15.31 Similarly, laws to prevent capitalists from hiring young children (e.g. education clauses of English Factory Acts) are often staggered: these laws can allow capitalists to continue to hire younger children for a number of years, which means that the maximum age at which a child can be hired is not legally enforceable until after many years since the initial introduction of the laws.32 In other words, if adults’ working day is restricted, children are used.
Consider laws aiming to restrict the working day. If there are n number of workers and an average worker works for h hour, then there is (n * h) hour of work from which surplus value will be extracted. But if, thanks to the laws that shorten the working day, the average worker works for h - x hour (where x > 0), then the total amount of work possible is n(h — x)— where n(h — x) < (n * h). This means that capital has access to less amount of total labour time of society. So n (number of available workers) has to be increased. One way of doing this is to use those sections of the working class which have not been hitherto used: children and women (and peasants and semi-proletarians as a part of the latent surplus population).”翻译为中文

除了资本的共同和相互冲突的利益之外，国家的亲工人干预背后还有国家“自身”的利益。如果普通民众在恶劣条件下生活和工作，如果他们的身体健康受损，从例如战争制造（后者，当然，最终也符合资本家阶级的利益）的角度来看，这对国家来说是不利的。资本主义国家需要大量健壮的人员参与战场，就像资本主义雇主需要他们参与工作场所一样。20 一些国家行为者的亲穷人态度也可能在一定限度内促成人们获得一些让步。当然，这些让步是由人民（工人和小规模生产者）的税收还是由资本家（和大地主）挪用的剩余部分资助的，这是一个有待商榷的问题。

3 国家亲工人干预的局限性是什么？为什么？
过去两节中所述应被视为一种趋势：在特定条件下，国家倾向于给予一些让步，但是否实际上会这样做，以及让步的充分性如何，是一个偶然的问题。实际上，以亲工人干预的形式承诺的通常远大于实际提供的。21 国家限制资本主义对人们产生不利影响的程度是有限的。亲工人政策已经到位，但它们往往没有得到充分实施。22

通过观察社会工资可以看到国家只提供有限的福利。在一些富裕国家（澳大利亚、加拿大、德国、瑞典和英国），社会工资或总体净补贴通常是正值（但非常小的正数），23 而在美国即使在繁荣年份通常也是负值（即，净税收），“这意味着工资和薪金领取者支付的税款多于他们所获得的 — 他们帮助减少了任何现有的财政赤字”。相比之下，“在同一时期的瑞典，净社会工资大致为零，表明其慷慨的社会福利支出实际上是自我融资的”（第538页）。24 一项较新的研究得出了类似的结论：
[在几乎所有发达资本主义国家中，[他们的]社会支出（特别是构成劳动福利或总社会工资的部分）必然来自总劳动收入，即来自个人所得税、社会保障缴款和其他对劳动征收的个人和财产税。只要假设间接企业税或消费税被认为是对劳动征收的，那么它们就财政上支持并通常超过即使在福利国家更发达的发达资本主义国家中的总社会工资的剩余部分。因此，如果我们假设间接税部分由劳动支付，那么在美国，净社会工资在整个战后时期显著为负（GDP的-6.0%到-7.5%），而在其他国家则从适度负值到接近零。
（Maniatis, 2014: 33）
工人阶级确实只从国家那里获得有限的福利，相对于资本家阶级所获得的援助。有趣的是，劳动力在国民收入中的份额一直在减少：在发达国家，从1970年的大约55%下降到2015年的大约40%，在新兴市场和发展中经济体，从1990年代中期的大约50%下降到2015年的不到40%（Roberts, 2017）。

即使对工人施加的资本主义过度剥削（极低工资，极长工作时间）的规制符合资本家的长期利益，后者仍然不接受这一点，“除非‘在议会的一般法案压力下’”（马克思，1887：315）。当资本家不情愿地让步，批准诸如“对劳动时间的强制性规定”等事项时，他们让步不多（同上）。亲工人干预只是“从资本中榨取的微薄让步”（马克思，1887：319），在英国工厂法案的情况下，它被“限制在将初等教育与工厂工作结合起来”（同上）。注意这是在资本主义相对于其当前终结衰退阶段的进步阶段的情况。国家确实继续未能执行其自己的立法。例如，它未能执行劳动者作为劳动力的卖家的自由：数以百万计的非自由劳动者，包括儿童，以奴隶般的条件工作。最低工资政策也未得到执行，而在执行的地方，通常，政府立法的最低工资低于集体协议设定的工资底线（Boeri, 2012）。25

总的来说，只要国家支持资本的长期利益，这种干预更有可能发生当（a）人民反抗剥削的影响和（b）统治阶级成分之间存在冲突时，这意味着（c）工人阶级的利益与资本家阶级的某些成分的利益相一致。国家或国家行为者的自身利益和观念也发挥作用。然而，国家未能以显著和持久的方式满足工人的需求。

资本家的能动性
尽管亲工人干预是由于资本的长期利益和阶级斗争，这些干预的性质和范围也受到资本家阶级对这些干预的回应的影响。资本家阶级旨在在这些政策实施后稀释和/或抵消亲工人政策的实际影响。他们以多种方式这样做。

为了保持自己的竞争优势，一些企业主抱怨如果某项立法不适用于所有企业，它将使那些不受其覆盖的企业受益。26 资本家还以它违背工人利益为由反对福利立法，因此他们动员特别是右翼政治家为他们辩护。

右翼的论点是当劳动者对他们的劳动力设定过高的保留价格时就会出现失业。劳动者拒绝在某一最低工资以下工作时会制造失业！当福利过于慷慨时通常会出现这种情况！因此，摆脱失业的最佳方式是将福利降至零。27
（哈维，2010a：281）
当立法强加于资本家时，他们包括通过法庭和政党以它是“干涉资本剥削权利”的理由对此进行抗议。个别资本家将他们的私有财产权视为神圣不可侵犯，因此对他们的温和行动被用来产生革命幽灵的夸张灾难性概念：“他们[资本家]把工厂检查员称为一种革命专员”（马克思，1887：188）。28 资本家将某些稍微改革倾向的自由派（例如尼赫鲁、奥巴马、拜登）视为社会主义者甚至马克思主义同情者的传统，以及使普通民众将其视为这种观点继续。这是恐惧社会主义/左翼的同样传统，将美国的民主党视为属于左翼。将公然属于资产阶级的观点和行动视为马克思主义、社会主义和左翼，然后将其呈现为对一个国家所代表的一切构成威胁的一种方式，是预防/避免任何亲工人措施的绝佳方式。

当存在限制资本家的法律时，资本家可以绕过这些法律或使国家难以实施它们，鉴于他们对生产和交换过程的控制。毕竟，工作场所是资本家私人立法的地点。这是资本家专制控制的地点，部分需要以对抗潜在和实际的工人内部抵抗。考虑资本家对限制工作日长度、使用童工等立法的反应。资本家采取多种机制来绕过亲工人法律。

首先，资本家对劳动过程进行改变。例如，他们增加了工作速度，导致工人受伤增加。资本在国家层面上或多或少不受惩罚。29 他们引入接力制度：个别（儿童）工人在一天24小时的不同时间点上班，这使得国家官员（检查员）难以监督任何工人是否工作超过法律允许的时间。30

其次，对劳动过程的特定形式的改变是诉诸技术解决方案（机械化），这对工人有两种不利影响。一方面，它是对工人斗争的一种武器：机器取代了工人，削弱了他们的议价能力。此外，通过“使妇女和儿童过度加入到工人队伍中，机械......打破了制造业时期男性操作工继续对资本专制的抵抗”（马克思，1887：276）。此外，资本使用更有效的机器在缩短的工作日内挤压出和较长的工作日一样多的劳动。

第三，资本家诉诸空间固定或威胁这样做。如果在一个国家实施了限制资本家剥削自由的法律，资本家威胁将他们的资本转移到其他地方，以便“恐吓”国家和工人。

第四，资本家诉诸所谓的时间固定。限制资本家剥削自由的法律的实施是分阶段的。例如，如果国家寻求提高最低工资，增加的过程会持续很长时间。在美国，提高工资法案建议将联邦最低工资从目前的7.25美元逐步提高到15美元：2021年：9.50美元；2022年：11美元；2023年：12.50美元；2024年：14美元；2025年：15美元。31 同样，禁止资本家雇佣年幼儿童的法律（例如英国工厂法案的教育条款）通常也是分阶段的：这些法律可以允许资本家在多年内继续雇佣更年轻的儿童，这意味着直到许多年后才能法律实施儿童可以被雇佣的最大年龄。32 换句话说，如果成年人的工作日受到限制，儿童就会被使用。

考虑旨在限制工作日的法律。如果有n个工人和平均每个工人工作h小时，那么总共有(n * h)小时的工作可以从中提取剩余价值。但如果由于缩短工作日的法律，平均工人工作h - x小时（其中x > 0），那么可能的总工作量是n(h - x) - 其中n(h - x) < (n * h)。这意味着资本可以获取的总劳动时间减少。因此，n（可用工人数量）必须增加。实现这一目标的一种方式是使用迄今未被使用的工人阶级的那些部分：儿童和妇女（以及农民和半无产者作为潜在剩余人口的一部分）。

请将“The state form and functioning
The nature of the functioning of the state itself limits the effectivity of proworker policies. Given that pro-worker policies will adversely affect capitalists, various processes (loopholes) are put in place to restrict the positive impact of good policies on workers. Political representatives of capital within the state who, more or less, work on behalf of capital and who share ideas that reflect capitalist interests make every effort, along with capitalists, to dilute the proworker laws and/or block their proper implementation. Every law is designed in such a way that it has many exceptions, which weaken the law.33 Pro-worker laws can also be mutually contradictory thanks to the fact that they partly reflect the contradictor interests of capital and labour. For example, one law shortens the working day, and another law makes available more children for exploitation by lowering the minimum wage a child can work.
